news_summary: Recent breakthroughs in pain relief, anti-aging, assistive technology, and innovative vaccines offer promising advancements for arthritis, age-related health, neuromuscular diseases, and infectious disease prevention.
news_summary_fr: Les récentes avancées en matière de traitement de la douleur, de lutte contre le vieillissement, de technologies d'assistance et de vaccins innovants ouvrent des perspectives prometteuses pour le traitement de l'arthrite, la santé liée au vieillissement, les maladies neuromusculaires et la prévention des maladies infectieuses.
news_summary_es: Los recientes avances en el alivio del dolor, el antienvejecimiento, la tecnología de apoyo y las vacunas innovadoras ofrecen perspectivas prometedoras para el tratamiento de la artritis, la salud relacionada con la edad, las enfermedades neuromusculares y la prevención de enfermedades infecciosas.

articles.title: Scientists discover simple way to relieve arthritis pain without pills or surgery
articles.summary: A surprisingly simple walking tweak may offer new hope for millions living with knee osteoarthritis. In a year-long clinical trial, researchers found that slightly changing the angle of a person’s foot while walking reduced knee pain as effectively as common medications — and even slowed cartilage damage inside the joint.
articles.content:
Nearly one in four adults over 40 lives with painful osteoarthritis, a condition that can make everyday movement difficult and is one of the major causes of adult disability. The disease gradually wears down the cartilage that cushions joints. Once that damage occurs, doctors currently cannot reverse it. Treatment usually focuses on pain relief, with joint replacement becoming an option when symptoms become severe.

A clinical trial from researchers at the University of Utah, New York University, and Stanford University points to a different possibility: changing the way a person walks.

A Personalized Shift in Foot Angle

In the yearlong randomized controlled trial, people with knee osteoarthritis were trained to make a small, personalized change in the angle of their foot while walking. The result was striking. Participants who received the real gait retraining reported pain relief comparable to medication, and MRI scans suggested they had less knee cartilage deterioration than people in the placebo group.

The study, published in The Lancet Rheumatology, was co led by Scott Uhlrich of the University of Utah's John and Marcia Price College of Engineering. According to the researchers, it was the first placebo controlled study to show that a biomechanical intervention could help treat osteoarthritis symptoms and potentially slow joint damage.

"We've known that for people with osteoarthritis, higher loads in their knee accelerate progression, and that changing the foot angle can reduce knee load," said Uhlrich, an assistant professor of mechanical engineering. "So the idea of a biomechanical intervention is not new, but there have not been randomized, placebo-controlled studies to show that they're effective."

Why the Treatment Had To Be Customized

The study focused on people with mild to moderate osteoarthritis in the medial compartment of the knee, which is the inner side of the joint. This area usually carries more weight than the outer side, making it a common site for knee osteoarthritis.

But there is a key challenge: the best walking adjustment is not the same for everyone. Some people reduce knee loading by turning their toes slightly inward. Others benefit more from pointing them outward. For some, the wrong change can fail to help or even increase stress on the painful part of the knee.

"Previous trials prescribed the same intervention to all individuals, resulting in some individuals not reducing, or even increasing, their joint loading," Uhlrich said. "We used a personalized approach to selecting each individual's new walking pattern, which improved how much individuals could offload their knee and likely contributed to the positive effect on pain and cartilage that we saw."

That point has become even more important as related research continues to show that foot angle changes can affect knee forces differently depending on the person, the joint, and the walking pattern. A 2024 study in Bioengineering, for example, found that inward and outward foot rotation affected different peaks of knee loading, while not significantly increasing ankle joint moments in the group studied. Other research has also shown that foot progression angle can be measured outside the lab with wearable sensors, supporting the idea that future versions of this approach could be easier to deliver in real life.

How the Trial Worked

During their first two visits, participants received a baseline MRI and walked on a pressure sensitive treadmill while motion capture cameras measured the mechanics of their gait. The researchers used those data to determine whether each person would benefit more from turning the toes inward or outward, and whether a 5° or 10° adjustment would be best.

This screening also identified people who were unlikely to benefit because none of the tested foot angle changes reduced loading in their knees. Those participants were excluded from the trial. The researchers noted that including such people in earlier studies may have helped explain why previous results on pain were less clear.

Of the 68 participants enrolled, half were assigned to the real gait retraining group. The other half received a sham treatment designed to control for the placebo effect. In the sham group, participants were assigned foot angles that matched their natural walking pattern. In the intervention group, each participant received the foot angle change that produced the greatest reduction in knee loading.

Training the New Walking Pattern

Both groups returned to the lab for six weekly training sessions. During these sessions, participants walked on a treadmill while wearing a device on the shin that provided vibration feedback. The vibrations helped them keep their assigned foot angle while walking.

After the six week training period, participants were encouraged to practice the walking pattern for at least 20 minutes each day. The goal was for the movement to become automatic. Follow up visits showed that, on average, participants stayed within one degree of their prescribed foot angle.

After one year, participants reported their knee pain levels and underwent a second MRI so researchers could measure changes in cartilage health.

"The reported decrease in pain over the placebo group was somewhere between what you'd expect from an over-the-counter medication, like ibuprofen, and a narcotic, like oxycontin," Uhlrich said. "With the MRIs, we also saw slower degradation of a marker of cartilage health in the intervention group, which was quite exciting."

A Drug Free Option for a Long Treatment Gap

For some participants, one of the most appealing parts of the approach was that it did not require pills, surgery, braces, or a device worn all day. One participant said: "I don't have to take a drug or wear a device…it's just a part of my body now that will be with me for the rest of my days, so that I'm thrilled with."

That long term adherence could be one of the intervention's biggest strengths. Many people develop osteoarthritis decades before they are candidates for joint replacement. During that time, they may rely heavily on pain medications and other symptom management strategies.

"Especially for people in their 30's, 40's, or 50's, osteoarthritis could mean decades of pain management before they're recommended for a joint replacement," Uhrlich said. "This intervention could help fill that large treatment gap."

A 2026 conference abstract in Osteoarthritis and Cartilage also highlighted continued interest in placebo controlled trials of foot progression angle retraining, underscoring that researchers are still trying to determine which gait strategies work best and for whom. However, this area remains under active study, and the 2025 Lancet Rheumatology trial is still one of the strongest clinical demonstrations of a personalized approach.

Why Patients Should Not Try This Alone

Although the findings are promising, the researchers emphasized that this is not a simple "turn your toes in" or "turn your toes out" recommendation. The benefit depended on careful measurement and personalization. For some people, the wrong adjustment could increase stress on the knee rather than reduce it.

That is why the process still needs to be simplified before it can be used widely in clinics. The motion capture system used to prescribe each person's walking change is expensive and time consuming. The research team envisions a future version that could be delivered through physical therapy, with retraining taking place during normal walks rather than only inside a lab.

"We and others have developed technology that could be used to both personalize and deliver this intervention in a clinical setting using mobile sensors, like smartphone video and a 'smart shoe'," Uhlrich said. Future studies of this approach are needed before the intervention can be made widely available to the public.

Those interested in participating in future studies can contact Uhlrich's Movement Bioengineering Lab by filling out this web form.

The study, titled "Personalised gait retraining for medial compartment knee osteoarthritis: a randomised controlled trial," was published in The Lancet Rheumatology. Co lead authors are Valentina Mazzoli of NYU's Department of Radiology and Julie Kolesar of Stanford's Human Performance Lab. Coauthors include Amy Silder, Andrea Finlay, Feliks Kogan, Garry Gold, Scott Delp and Gary Beaupre of Stanford and the VA Palo Alto Medical Center. The research was supported by federal research grants from the Department of Veterans Affairs, National Institutes of Health and National Science Foundation.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant clinical trial demonstrating a personalized, non-drug, non-surgical intervention that effectively reduces knee osteoarthritis pain and slows cartilage damage, benefiting millions globally. The study is well-detailed, with clear evidence of positive real-world impact and potential for broad application, addressing a major cause of disability with a novel biomechanical approach.
articles.personality_title: Simple walking change eases knee arthritis pain and slows joint damage
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context**
Knee osteoarthritis is a common condition that causes pain and difficulty moving. It happens when the cartilage that cushions the knee joint wears away. Today, treatments focus on relieving pain, and surgery is an option only for severe cases.

**What happened**
A clinical trial by researchers from the University of Utah, New York University, and Stanford University tested a new approach. They taught people with knee arthritis to slightly change the angle of their foot while walking. This personalized change, either turning toes inward or outward by a small amount, reduced pain as well as medicine and slowed cartilage damage. Participants trained over six weeks using a device that gave feedback, then kept practicing for a year.

**Impact**
This is the first study to prove that a simple, customized walking adjustment can reduce arthritis pain and protect knee cartilage. Unlike drugs or surgery, this method uses the body's natural movement and can be done without daily devices or pills. It offers a new option for people who face years of pain before surgery might be needed.

**What's next step**
Researchers want to make this treatment easier to use outside the lab, possibly with smartphone videos or smart shoes. More studies will help find the best walking adjustments for different people and bring this approach to clinics.

**One-sentence takeaway**
A small, personalized change in how people walk can reduce knee arthritis pain and slow joint damage without drugs or surgery.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un simple changement de marche soulage l’arthrite du genou et ralentit les dommages articulaires
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte**
L’arthrose du genou est une maladie fréquente qui cause douleur et difficultés à bouger. Elle survient lorsque le cartilage qui protège l’articulation s’use. Aujourd’hui, les traitements visent surtout à soulager la douleur, et la chirurgie est réservée aux cas graves.

**Ce qui s’est passé**
Une étude menée par des chercheurs de l’Université de l’Utah, de l’Université de New York et de Stanford a testé une nouvelle méthode. Ils ont appris aux personnes atteintes d’arthrose à modifier légèrement l’angle de leur pied en marchant. Ce changement personnalisé, en tournant légèrement les orteils vers l’intérieur ou l’extérieur, a réduit la douleur autant que les médicaments et a ralenti l’usure du cartilage. Les participants ont suivi un entraînement de six semaines avec un appareil de retour tactile, puis ont continué à pratiquer pendant un an.

**Impact**
C’est la première étude à montrer qu’un simple ajustement de la marche, adapté à chaque personne, peut diminuer la douleur de l’arthrose et protéger le cartilage du genou. Contrairement aux médicaments ou à la chirurgie, cette méthode utilise le mouvement naturel du corps et ne nécessite pas d’appareils ou de pilules quotidiens. Elle offre une nouvelle option pour les personnes qui souffrent pendant des années avant de pouvoir envisager une opération.

**Prochaine étape**
Les chercheurs souhaitent rendre ce traitement plus facile à utiliser hors du laboratoire, grâce à des vidéos sur smartphone ou des chaussures intelligentes. D’autres études aideront à déterminer les meilleurs ajustements de marche pour chacun et à proposer cette méthode en clinique.

**Résumé en une phrase**
Un petit changement personnalisé dans la façon de marcher peut réduire la douleur de l’arthrose du genou et ralentir les dommages articulaires sans médicaments ni chirurgie.
articles.personality_title_es: Un simple cambio al caminar alivia el dolor de artritis de rodilla y frena el daño articular
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto**
La osteoartritis de rodilla es una enfermedad común que causa dolor y dificulta el movimiento. Ocurre cuando el cartílago que protege la articulación se desgasta. Actualmente, los tratamientos se centran en aliviar el dolor, y la cirugía se reserva para casos graves.

**Qué pasó**
Un ensayo clínico realizado por investigadores de la Universidad de Utah, la Universidad de Nueva York y Stanford probó un nuevo método. Enseñaron a personas con artritis a cambiar ligeramente el ángulo del pie al caminar. Este cambio personalizado, girando un poco los dedos hacia adentro o hacia afuera, redujo el dolor tanto como los medicamentos y ralentizó el daño al cartílago. Los participantes entrenaron durante seis semanas con un dispositivo que les daba retroalimentación, y continuaron practicando durante un año.

**Impacto**
Este es el primer estudio que demuestra que un simple ajuste personalizado en la forma de caminar puede reducir el dolor de la artritis y proteger el cartílago de la rodilla. A diferencia de los medicamentos o la cirugía, este método usa el movimiento natural del cuerpo y no requiere dispositivos o pastillas diarias. Ofrece una nueva opción para quienes sufren durante años antes de necesitar una operación.

**Próximos pasos**
Los investigadores quieren hacer que este tratamiento sea más fácil de usar fuera del laboratorio, posiblemente con videos en smartphones o zapatos inteligentes. Más estudios ayudarán a encontrar los mejores ajustes para cada persona y a llevar este método a las clínicas.

**Resumen en una frase**
Un pequeño cambio personalizado en la forma de caminar puede reducir el dolor de la artritis de rodilla y frenar el daño articular sin medicamentos ni cirugía.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of two stylized footprints on a softly glowing path, each footprint subtly angled differently to symbolize personalized walking adjustments, surrounded by gentle, abstract representations of knee joints made of smooth, cushioned shapes that suggest healing and reduced pain, all rendered in natural, muted earth tones.

articles.title: “Zombie cells” aren’t always bad and that could transform anti-aging medicine
articles.summary: Scientists are uncovering a surprising truth about aging cells: some may damage the body, while others help protect it. The discovery is fueling a new wave of precision anti-aging therapies aimed at removing only the harmful “zombie” cells without disrupting the body’s natural repair systems.
articles.content:
Aging research is entering a new phase as scientists rethink one of the body's most mysterious cell types: senescent cells, often nicknamed "zombie cells." While these aging cells have long been viewed as harmful drivers of inflammation and disease, new findings suggest the story is far more complicated. In some cases, senescent cells may actually help protect and repair the body.

A new review published May 4, 2026, in Volume 18 of Aging-US explores how cellular senescence shapes aging throughout the body and examines the growing push toward precision anti-aging therapies. The study, titled "Cellular senescence: from pathogenic mechanisms to precision anti-aging interventions," was led by first author Jian Deng and corresponding author Dong Yang from the Department of Targeting Therapy and Immunology, Cancer Center, West China Hospital, Sichuan University, Chengdu, Sichuan, China.

How Senescent Cells Influence Aging

Senescent cells are cells that have permanently stopped dividing. Scientists have traditionally considered them harmful because they accumulate with age and release inflammatory molecules that can damage nearby tissue. These cells have been linked to a wide range of age-related diseases and declining organ function.

However, the review highlights mounting evidence that senescent cells are not universally harmful. Some appear to serve important biological functions, including supporting wound healing, maintaining tissue balance, and helping guide embryonic development.

Researchers describe how senescence develops across many organs, including the liver, lungs, kidneys, heart, brain, skin, and fat tissue. In each of these systems, cells can become senescent due to factors such as oxidative stress, mitochondrial dysfunction, DNA damage, chronic inflammation, metabolic stress, telomere shortening, ultraviolet radiation, and environmental pollution.

The review also explains that senescent cells build up in many specialized cell types, including hepatocytes, endothelial cells, fibroblasts, macrophages, astrocytes, and epithelial cells. Once these cells accumulate, they can interfere with normal tissue structure and contribute to chronic illness.

Scientists Discover Senescent Cells Behave Very Differently

One of the review's central conclusions is that senescent cells are highly diverse and should not be treated as a single uniform population. Depending on where they are located and how they interact with surrounding tissue, senescent cells can either help or harm the body.

Some senescent cells may limit fibrosis and assist tissue repair, while others appear to fuel chronic inflammation, metabolic disorders, tissue degeneration, and even cancer progression. Because of this complexity, anti-aging researchers are increasingly moving away from strategies that attempt to eliminate all senescent cells.

"Based on these insights, this review summarizes the induction mechanisms of cellular senescence and the subsequent evolution of their functional phenotypes across diverse tissues."

Instead, scientists are now exploring more selective approaches designed to target only the harmful senescent cell populations while preserving beneficial ones.

Precision Anti-Aging Therapies

The review outlines several emerging anti-aging strategies aimed at controlling senescent cells more precisely.

Early senolytic drugs such as dasatinib, quercetin, and fisetin were developed to destroy senescent cells by disrupting the survival pathways that keep them alive. More recent therapies are becoming increasingly sophisticated.

Some researchers are now investigating CAR-T cell immunotherapies that can recognize markers found on senescent cells and selectively remove them. Other approaches involve "senomorphic" therapies, which attempt to reduce the harmful inflammatory signals known as the senescence-associated secretory phenotype (SASP) without killing the cells themselves.

A major concept discussed in the review is "precision geroprotection." This strategy focuses on identifying and eliminating only maladaptive senescent cells while preserving cells that still contribute to tissue repair and stability.

Scientists believe emerging technologies such as single-cell omics, lineage tracing, and spatial profiling may help reveal the distinct subtypes of senescent cells and identify safer therapeutic targets.

Challenges Facing Anti-Aging Treatments

Despite growing excitement in the field, researchers caution that major challenges remain before senescence-targeting therapies can be widely used in medicine.

One problem is the lack of highly specific biomarkers that can reliably distinguish harmful senescent cells from beneficial ones. Researchers also face difficulties delivering therapies precisely to target tissues while avoiding unintended damage to healthy organs.

The review warns that broad removal of senescent cells could potentially interfere with tissue repair, immune surveillance, blood vessel stability, and structural integrity in sensitive organs such as the heart, lungs, and brain.

Scientists also still do not fully understand how senescent cell populations change over time in different organs, making it difficult to predict the long-term effects of treatment.

A More Personalized Future for Anti-Aging Research

Overall, the review presents a more nuanced understanding of cellular senescence and aging. Rather than viewing all senescent cells as dangerous, researchers increasingly believe the future of anti-aging medicine may depend on distinguishing harmful cells from beneficial ones.

The authors propose a more individualized approach centered on prevention, functional analysis, and precision intervention. As anti-aging science continues to evolve, these strategies could eventually support healthier aging while reducing the risks associated with indiscriminate senescent cell removal.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific advancement in understanding senescent cells, revealing that some aging cells have protective roles. This nuanced insight is driving the development of precision anti-aging therapies that could improve health outcomes for the aging population by selectively targeting harmful cells while preserving beneficial ones. The research has broad implications for aging-related diseases and represents a meaningful breakthrough in health science with potential large-scale benefits.
articles.personality_title: New discovery shows some aging cells protect the body, changing anti-aging treatments
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have been studying senescent cells, often called "zombie cells," which are old cells that stop dividing. These cells build up as people age and were mostly thought to cause damage and diseases.

**What happened** – A new review published in May 2026 by researchers from Sichuan University in China found that not all senescent cells are harmful. Some actually help the body heal wounds, keep tissues balanced, and support growth before birth. The study explains that senescent cells behave differently depending on where they are and what they do.

**Impact** – This discovery changes how scientists think about aging. Instead of trying to remove all senescent cells, new treatments aim to carefully remove only the harmful ones, while keeping the helpful cells that protect and repair the body. This approach could make anti-aging medicines safer and more effective.

**What's next step** – Researchers are developing new tools to identify which senescent cells are harmful and which are helpful. They are testing advanced therapies like special immune cells (CAR-T cells) and drugs that reduce harmful signals without killing cells. Scientists hope this will lead to personalized treatments that help people age healthier.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have learned that some aging cells protect the body, leading to smarter anti-aging treatments that target only harmful cells while preserving helpful ones.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle découverte montre que certaines cellules vieillissantes protègent le corps, changeant les traitements anti-âge
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient les cellules sénescentes, souvent appelées « cellules zombies », qui sont des cellules âgées ayant cessé de se diviser. Ces cellules s’accumulent avec l’âge et étaient surtout considérées comme nuisibles et responsables de maladies.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une nouvelle revue publiée en mai 2026 par des chercheurs de l’Université du Sichuan en Chine a découvert que toutes les cellules sénescentes ne sont pas nuisibles. Certaines aident en fait le corps à guérir les blessures, à maintenir l’équilibre des tissus et à soutenir la croissance avant la naissance. L’étude explique que ces cellules se comportent différemment selon leur localisation et leur rôle.

**Impact** – Cette découverte change la façon dont les scientifiques considèrent le vieillissement. Au lieu d’essayer d’éliminer toutes les cellules sénescentes, les nouveaux traitements visent à supprimer uniquement les cellules nuisibles, tout en conservant celles qui protègent et réparent le corps. Cette approche pourrait rendre les médicaments anti-âge plus sûrs et plus efficaces.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs développent de nouveaux outils pour identifier quelles cellules sénescentes sont nuisibles et lesquelles sont bénéfiques. Ils testent des thérapies avancées comme des cellules immunitaires spéciales (cellules CAR-T) et des médicaments qui réduisent les signaux nuisibles sans tuer les cellules. L’objectif est d’arriver à des traitements personnalisés pour un vieillissement en meilleure santé.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les scientifiques ont découvert que certaines cellules vieillissantes protègent le corps, ce qui ouvre la voie à des traitements anti-âge plus précis ciblant uniquement les cellules nuisibles tout en préservant les cellules utiles.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo descubrimiento muestra que algunas células envejecidas protegen el cuerpo, cambiando tratamientos anti-envejecimiento
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han estado estudiando las células senescentes, llamadas a menudo "células zombis", que son células viejas que dejan de dividirse. Estas células se acumulan con la edad y se pensaba que causaban daño y enfermedades.

**Qué pasó** – Una nueva revisión publicada en mayo de 2026 por investigadores de la Universidad de Sichuan en China encontró que no todas las células senescentes son dañinas. Algunas en realidad ayudan al cuerpo a curar heridas, mantener el equilibrio de los tejidos y apoyar el crecimiento antes del nacimiento. El estudio explica que estas células se comportan de manera diferente según dónde están y qué hacen.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento cambia la forma en que los científicos piensan sobre el envejecimiento. En lugar de eliminar todas las células senescentes, los nuevos tratamientos buscan eliminar solo las dañinas, mientras conservan las que son útiles para proteger y reparar el cuerpo. Esto podría hacer que los medicamentos anti-envejecimiento sean más seguros y efectivos.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores están creando nuevas herramientas para identificar qué células senescentes son dañinas y cuáles son beneficiosas. Están probando terapias avanzadas como células inmunes especiales (células CAR-T) y medicamentos que reducen señales dañinas sin matar las células. Esperan que esto lleve a tratamientos personalizados para un envejecimiento más saludable.

**Frase clave** – Los científicos descubrieron que algunas células envejecidas protegen el cuerpo, lo que permite tratamientos anti-envejecimiento más inteligentes que solo atacan las células dañinas y preservan las útiles.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a vibrant, glowing network of diverse cells—some depicted as gentle, nurturing figures weaving golden threads of repair and protection, others as shadowed shapes emitting subtle wisps of inflammation—interconnected within a softly illuminated, abstract human organ landscape, using natural earthy tones and soft pastels to symbolize the complex balance of senescent cells in aging and healing.

articles.title: Daily briefing: Wearable robot could help kids with neuromuscular disease stand
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 21 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01656-9A robotic device helps children with spinal muscular atrophy build up strength in their knees. Plus, a tough peer-review process could lead to a more-cited paper and chemists’ mission to replace ‘forever chemicals’.
articles.content:
Hello Nature readers, would you like to get this Briefing in your inbox free every day? Sign up here.

To help children to engage with the challenging sessions using a new robotic device, researchers gamified the training so that the kids saw themselves kicking a ball as they extended their leg. (Yuebing Li et al/Nature)

A lightweight, wearable robotic device can help children living with a rare condition called spinal muscular atrophy (SMA) improve their knee function, which could help them stand unassisted. The device provides resistance training tailored to the individual needs of children who are unable to walk because of SMA. After using the robot for six weeks, six children — aged between 6 and 10 years — could stand from a lower sitting angle, had 20% bigger quadriceps, and could generate more than twice as much force when bending their knees, compared to pre-training assessments.

Nature | 5 min read

Reference: Nature paper

Papers that are put through the wringer during the peer-review process go on to have a higher impact in science than those that sail through it. Researchers used artificial intelligence to analyse the peer-review correspondence associated with a selection of papers published in Nature Communications. They found that papers that elicited stronger criticism from reviewers and required more-extensive revisions received more citations than did papers that drew lighter comments. A higher ‘quality’ of reviewer comments — those that were more specific and well-reasoned — was also associated with a boost in citations to the published product.

Nature | 5 min read

Reference: arXiv preprint (not peer reviewed)

An extra-long snooze after a short night’s sleep appears to offset some of the negative health effects associated with sleep deprivation. Researchers found that people who caught up on sleep the night after a brief slumber — termed ‘sleep rebound’ — were less likely to die in the eight years following the study period than were people who didn’t. They also found that most sleep rebounders didn’t wait until the weekend to sleep in. Instead, they tried to compensate for lost sleep as soon as possible.

Science | 4 min read

Reference: Nature Communications paper

Infographic of the week

Source: Nature Index

According to the Nature Index database of research articles in a selected group of scientific journals, China has increased its chemistry research output by almost 350% since 2015. Its contribution as of 2025 is higher than the top 50 other countries combined. (Nature Index | 4 min read)

Features & opinion

In East Africa, giant ants are the new flavour of the month for poachers. The queens of one species, Messor cephalotes, are in particular demand because their striking colouring is sought after by collectors. Removing a queen can spell disaster for its colony, which relies on it to produce more offspring, and the collapse of ant populations can have serious ecological consequences, experts say. Countries such as Kenya are trying to clamp down on trafficking, but the insects’ size, the complexity of species identification and limited monitoring capacity make the ant trade hard to detect.

Nature Africa | 6 min read

Fluorine-containing compounds are everywhere, from weatherproof clothing and non-stick cookware to batteries and computer chips. But the strong chemical bonds that make fluorine products so useful are also their greatest flaw. Per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFASs) — ‘forever chemicals’ — are toxic, do not break down naturally, and making them is a hazardous, energy-intensive process. Chemists are rising to the challenge, investigating alternatives to fluorochemicals and ways to recycle PFAS waste. Driving the field forward are proposed PFAS bans, such as the one being considered in the European Union.

Nature Index | 13 min read

Observers focused on the threats to publicly funded science in the United States should widen their view, argues virologist Humberto Debat. “From the perspective of a scientist working in the Global South, what is happening in the US looks less like an anomaly and more like another addition to a growing list of countries where the scientific community is under siege,” he writes. For example, Debat points to Argentina, where he has a laboratory, and which has seen its scientific infrastructure dismantled by the administration of President Javier Milei. The international scientific community can no longer absorb these shocks on an ad-hoc basis, he argues: global scientific bodies and funders must get organized to shore up science during times of trouble.

eLife | 13 min read

QOTD
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a lightweight, wearable robotic device that significantly improves knee function in children with spinal muscular atrophy, enabling them to stand unassisted. This represents a meaningful, tangible health breakthrough with broad positive implications for children suffering from this rare neuromuscular disease. The article provides clear context, data on the improvements, and focuses on this single impactful innovation.
articles.personality_title: Wearable robot helps children with neuromuscular disease stand on their own
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Spinal muscular atrophy (SMA) is a rare disease that makes it hard for children to control their muscles, often preventing them from standing or walking. Building muscle strength in affected children is a big challenge.

**What happened** – Researchers created a lightweight, wearable robot that children with SMA can use to train their knees. The robot adds resistance while the kids move, helping their leg muscles get stronger. To make training fun, the device shows a game where children see themselves kicking a ball as they extend their legs. After six weeks of using this robot, six children aged 6 to 10 were able to stand up from lower sitting positions. Their thigh muscles grew 20% bigger, and they could bend their knees with more than twice the force they had before.

**Impact** – This device is important because it helps children who cannot walk to build real muscle strength and improve their ability to stand without help. Standing is a key step toward more independence and better quality of life. The combination of personalized resistance and engaging games makes the training easier and more effective for young users.

**What's next step** – Researchers will likely test the robot with more children and over longer periods to see how much it can help. They might also improve the games and adjust the device to support other muscle groups or conditions.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new wearable robot helps children with spinal muscular atrophy strengthen their knees and stand independently through fun, personalized training.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un robot portable aide les enfants atteints d’une maladie neuromusculaire à se tenir debout seuls
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’atrophie musculaire spinale (AMS) est une maladie rare qui rend difficile le contrôle des muscles chez les enfants, les empêchant souvent de se tenir debout ou de marcher. Renforcer les muscles chez ces enfants est un grand défi.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont créé un robot léger et portable que les enfants atteints d’AMS peuvent utiliser pour entraîner leurs genoux. Le robot offre une résistance pendant que les enfants bougent, ce qui aide leurs muscles des jambes à devenir plus forts. Pour rendre l’entraînement amusant, l’appareil affiche un jeu où les enfants se voient en train de frapper un ballon quand ils tendent leurs jambes. Après six semaines d’utilisation, six enfants âgés de 6 à 10 ans pouvaient se lever depuis une position assise plus basse. Leurs muscles des cuisses ont grossi de 20 %, et ils pouvaient plier les genoux avec plus du double de la force d’avant.

**Impact** – Cet appareil est important car il aide les enfants qui ne peuvent pas marcher à renforcer leurs muscles et à se tenir debout sans aide. Se tenir debout est un pas clé vers plus d’autonomie et une meilleure qualité de vie. La combinaison d’une résistance personnalisée et de jeux engageants rend l’entraînement plus facile et efficace pour les jeunes utilisateurs.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont probablement tester le robot avec plus d’enfants et sur des périodes plus longues pour mesurer ses bénéfices. Ils pourraient aussi améliorer les jeux et adapter l’appareil pour soutenir d’autres groupes musculaires ou maladies.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un nouveau robot portable aide les enfants atteints d’atrophie musculaire spinale à renforcer leurs genoux et à se tenir debout seuls grâce à un entraînement ludique et personnalisé.
articles.personality_title_es: Un robot portátil ayuda a niños con enfermedad neuromuscular a ponerse de pie solos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La atrofia muscular espinal (AME) es una enfermedad rara que dificulta que los niños controlen sus músculos, impidiéndoles a menudo ponerse de pie o caminar. Fortalecer los músculos en estos niños es un gran reto.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores crearon un robot ligero y portátil que los niños con AME pueden usar para entrenar sus rodillas. El robot ofrece resistencia mientras se mueven, ayudando a que los músculos de las piernas se fortalezcan. Para hacer el entrenamiento divertido, el dispositivo muestra un juego donde los niños se ven pateando una pelota mientras extienden sus piernas. Después de seis semanas usando el robot, seis niños de entre 6 y 10 años pudieron ponerse de pie desde posiciones sentadas más bajas. Sus músculos del muslo crecieron un 20 % y pudieron doblar las rodillas con más del doble de fuerza que antes.

**Impacto** – Este dispositivo es importante porque ayuda a niños que no pueden caminar a fortalecer sus músculos y mejorar su capacidad para ponerse de pie sin ayuda. Ponerse de pie es un paso clave hacia más independencia y mejor calidad de vida. La combinación de resistencia personalizada y juegos entretenidos hace que el entrenamiento sea más fácil y efectivo para los niños.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores probablemente probarán el robot con más niños y por períodos más largos para ver cuánto puede ayudar. También podrían mejorar los juegos y ajustar el dispositivo para apoyar otros grupos musculares o condiciones.

**Resumen en una frase** – Un nuevo robot portátil ayuda a niños con atrofia muscular espinal a fortalecer sus rodillas y ponerse de pie solos mediante un entrenamiento divertido y personalizado.
articles.image_prompt: A gentle, detailed painting of a small, lightweight robotic exoskeleton softly embracing a child-sized silhouette seated with one leg extended, connected to a glowing, stylized knee joint that radiates strength and growth, set against a warm, simple background of soft earth tones and subtle hints of playful motion like a faint outline of a ball being kicked.

articles.title: Experimental mRNA vaccine may protect against multiple Ebola viruses
articles.summary: Tests with rodents suggest an mRNA vaccine in development offers protection against three strains of Ebola virus, including the one behind the current crisis
articles.content:
Tests with rodents suggest an mRNA vaccine in development offers protection against three strains of Ebola virus, including the one behind the current crisis

Health officials working to tackle Bundibugyo virus in the Democratic Republic of the Congo on 21 May Michel Lunanga/Getty Images

A new mRNA vaccine has been developed that may provide long-term protection against the deadliest viruses in the Ebola family – including the Bundibugyo strain currently spreading in two African countries.

Over 600 people are thought to have been infected with Bundibugyo virus in the Democratic Republic of the Congo and there have been two confirmed cases in Uganda, leading the World Health Organization to declare the outbreak a public health emergency of international concern.

Bundibugyo virus is a member of a group of pathogens known as orthoebolaviruses, which also includes the most common form of Ebola – the Zaire virus – and Sudan virus. All three can cause severe disease in humans.

Until now, Bundibugyo outbreaks have been rare compared with those of the Zaire strain, which infected over 28,000 people between 2014 and 2016. There are two approved vaccines for the Zaire virus, but none for the Bundibugyo or Sudan viruses.

Now, Yanfeng Yao at the Wuhan Institute of Virology in China and his colleagues say they have developed a vaccine that works in mice to protect against all three viruses.

Free newsletter Sign up to Eight Weeks to a Healthier You Your science-backed guide to the easy habits that will help you sleep well, stress less, eat smarter and age better. Sign up to newsletter

“The development of a broad-spectrum vaccine has the potential to efficiently mitigate outbreaks caused by multiple orthoebolaviruses,” they write in a new paper presenting their research.

The challenge for scientists trying to produce a vaccine effective for all three of these Ebola-family viruses is that they each carry different compounds, called glycoproteins, that are essential for infection. However, they all share the same nucleoproteins that package the virus’s genetic material.

To build their new vaccine, Yao and his team combined the mRNA encoding the glycoproteins of each virus – as well as the shared nucleoprotein – inside a single lipid nanoparticle, which is a sphere of fat molecules that protects the mRNA vaccine until it can reach the body’s cells.

Mice were administered the vaccine and monitored to see if their immune system responded, before being exposed to all three viruses. All immunised animals gained complete protection against infection by the Zaire and Sudan viruses and were conferred “strong” protection from Bundibugyo. Hamsters exposed to Sudan virus were also afforded complete protection by the vaccine.

The researchers say the trials show they have developed a broad-spectrum mRNA vaccine that effective defends against the Zaire, Sudan and Bundibugyo vaccines. But they also caution that the vaccine has only been tested on rodents so far, and plenty more work is needed to show it is safe and effective in humans.

Robert Cross at the University of Texas Medical Branch says he is “glad to see more creative next-generation [Ebola] vaccines being explored”.

However, he warns that testing in non-human primates is the gold standard for predicting efficacy in humans, and it will be difficult to get approval for a vaccine targeting multiple pathogens.

“It’s hard enough to get a vaccine approved for a specific virus. Getting to license with a multivalent vaccine has an arguably more complex path to approval,” says Cross.

Adrian Esterman at Adelaide University in Australia says it is a promising preclinical study, but a limitation is that the findings only apply to rodents.

“It is probably too early to give a firm timeframe for clinical use, but moving from this stage to human trials would usually take several years because further animal work – for example in primates – manufacturing development and safety testing are still needed,” he says.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on the development of a novel mRNA vaccine that offers broad protection against multiple deadly Ebola virus strains, addressing a significant public health threat with potential large-scale impact. The vaccine has shown promising results in animal tests, indicating a meaningful scientific breakthrough with the potential to improve outbreak control and save lives. Although still in preclinical stages, the detailed context and significance of the research meet the criteria for inspiring good news in health.
articles.personality_title: New mRNA vaccine shows promise against three Ebola viruses in animal tests
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Ebola viruses can cause deadly diseases and there are several types, including Zaire, Sudan, and Bundibugyo. While vaccines exist for the Zaire strain, no approved vaccines protect against Sudan or Bundibugyo. Recently, Bundibugyo virus has caused outbreaks in parts of Africa, raising concerns for public health.

**What happened** – Scientists in China developed an experimental mRNA vaccine that combines parts of all three Ebola viruses into one shot. They tested it on mice and hamsters, giving the animals the vaccine and then exposing them to the viruses. The vaccinated rodents were fully protected from the Zaire and Sudan viruses and had strong protection against Bundibugyo.

**Impact** – This vaccine is important because it could protect against multiple Ebola viruses with a single shot. Right now, no vaccine covers all three strains. Protecting against more than one virus can help control outbreaks faster and save more lives, especially in places where these viruses spread.

**What's next step** – The vaccine still needs to be tested on larger animals like monkeys and then in humans to make sure it is safe and works well. This process can take several years, and getting approval for a vaccine that targets multiple viruses is more complicated than for a single virus.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new mRNA vaccine has shown in animals that it can protect against three deadly Ebola viruses, offering hope for broader outbreak protection in the future.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouveau vaccin à ARNm prometteur contre trois virus Ebola testé sur des animaux
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les virus Ebola peuvent provoquer des maladies mortelles et il en existe plusieurs types, dont Zaire, Soudan et Bundibugyo. Bien qu’il existe des vaccins pour le virus Zaire, aucun vaccin approuvé ne protège contre les virus Soudan ou Bundibugyo. Récemment, le virus Bundibugyo a causé des épidémies en Afrique, ce qui suscite des inquiétudes pour la santé publique.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques en Chine ont développé un vaccin expérimental à ARNm qui combine des parties des trois virus Ebola en une seule injection. Ils l’ont testé sur des souris et des hamsters, qui ont été vaccinés puis exposés aux virus. Les rongeurs vaccinés ont été entièrement protégés contre les virus Zaire et Soudan et ont eu une forte protection contre Bundibugyo.

**Impact** – Ce vaccin est important car il pourrait protéger contre plusieurs virus Ebola avec une seule injection. Actuellement, aucun vaccin ne couvre les trois souches. Protéger contre plusieurs virus peut aider à contrôler plus rapidement les épidémies et sauver plus de vies, surtout dans les régions où ces virus se propagent.

**Prochaine étape** – Le vaccin doit encore être testé sur des animaux plus grands comme les singes, puis sur des humains pour vérifier sa sécurité et son efficacité. Ce processus peut prendre plusieurs années, et obtenir l’approbation d’un vaccin ciblant plusieurs virus est plus compliqué que pour un seul virus.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un nouveau vaccin à ARNm a montré chez les animaux qu’il peut protéger contre trois virus Ebola mortels, offrant l’espoir d’une protection plus large contre les épidémies à l’avenir.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva vacuna de ARNm muestra eficacia contra tres virus del Ébola en pruebas con animales
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los virus del Ébola pueden causar enfermedades mortales y existen varios tipos, incluidos Zaire, Sudán y Bundibugyo. Aunque hay vacunas para el virus Zaire, no hay vacunas aprobadas para proteger contra los virus Sudán o Bundibugyo. Recientemente, el virus Bundibugyo ha provocado brotes en África, lo que genera preocupación para la salud pública.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos en China desarrollaron una vacuna experimental de ARNm que combina partes de los tres virus del Ébola en una sola inyección. La probaron en ratones y hámsters, vacunándolos y luego exponiéndolos a los virus. Los roedores vacunados estuvieron completamente protegidos contra los virus Zaire y Sudán y tuvieron una fuerte protección contra Bundibugyo.

**Impacto** – Esta vacuna es importante porque podría proteger contra varios virus del Ébola con una sola dosis. Actualmente, no existe una vacuna que cubra las tres cepas. Proteger contra varios virus puede ayudar a controlar los brotes más rápido y salvar más vidas, especialmente en lugares donde estos virus se propagan.

**Próximos pasos** – La vacuna aún debe probarse en animales más grandes, como monos, y luego en humanos para asegurar que sea segura y efectiva. Este proceso puede tomar varios años, y obtener la aprobación para una vacuna que cubra varios virus es más complicado que para una sola.

**Resumen en una frase** – Una nueva vacuna de ARNm ha demostrado en animales que puede proteger contra tres virus mortales del Ébola, ofreciendo esperanza para una protección más amplia contra futuros brotes.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of three intertwined lipid nanoparticle spheres gently glowing with soft natural hues, each containing stylized molecular structures representing distinct Ebola virus glycoproteins and a shared nucleoprotein core, set against a calm, abstract background symbolizing protection and hope.
